claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand your situation. 

**Do you have a child or children you're claiming Child Benefit for?** If yes, please tell me:

1. **The name(s) of your child/children**
2. **Their age(s)** (or date of birth)

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** automatically qualify (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** are not eligible for Child Benefit

Please share these details so I can guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'd like to claim for my son Alex. He's 17 and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're looking to claim Child Benefit for your son Alex, who is 17 and lives with you.

Since Alex is 17 years old, he falls into the 16-19 age category. For children in this age group, eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex currently in any of the following situations?**

1. **Currently in approved education or training** (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs, or other approved courses)

2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last 20 weeks)

3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

Please let me know which applies to Alex's situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He recently left school and has signed up with the local careers service to figure out what he wants to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. So Alex recently left school and has signed up with the local careers service.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - registering with the local careers service may qualify Alex for an extension period! There's a **20-week extension** available for 16-17 year olds who have:
- Left approved education or training, AND
- Registered with a qualifying service (like a government careers service, which it sounds like Alex has done)

To confirm eligibility for this extension, I need to know:

**When did Alex leave school?** (What date or approximately how many weeks ago?)

This is important because the extension only lasts for 20 weeks from when he left education, or until he turns 18 (whichever comes first).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about a couple of months now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! A couple of months is approximately 8-10 weeks, which means Alex is well within the 20-week extension period. Since he left school recently and has registered with the local careers service, he qualifies for the extension.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's work situation.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week** (or not working at all)?

For young people aged 16-19 who aren't in approved education or training, they can't be working 24 hours or more per week to remain eligible for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at all right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's fine. Since Alex isn't working, he meets this requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question about Alex's situation:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?** Specifically:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or similar benefits claimed in his own name

(I need to confirm he's NOT receiving these benefits to continue with eligibility)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't get any benefits himself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's good for the claim. Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own right.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next, I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex enrolled in any type of apprenticeship?**

This is important because:
- Most apprenticeships (in England and Scotland) would disqualify him from Child Benefit
- Only Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales are approved
- If he's not in an apprenticeship at all, that's fine

What's Alex's situation regarding apprenticeships?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not doing an apprenticeship. He's just registered with the careers service while he decides what to do.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is not in an apprenticeship, he meets this requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status as the claimant.

**Do you normally live in the UK?** 

Or if not, do any of these special circumstances apply to you:
- You have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You're a Crown servant living abroad
- Other specific qualifying situations

For most people, simply living in the UK normally is sufficient. What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in the UK (in Manchester) means you meet the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives at home with you. **Is that correct - Alex lives with you?**

This would demonstrate that you're responsible for him, which is one of the key requirements for claiming Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - he lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex lives with you, you are clearly responsible for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. What's the situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since no one else is claiming for Alex, that's fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation. You've said he lives at home with you, but I need to confirm:

**Is Alex in any of these situations:**
1. Currently in hospital (as an in-patient)
2. In local authority care
3. Being fostered
4. None of the above - living normally at home with you

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: None of those - he's just living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Alex is living normally at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

For example, are they providing any financial support for his housing or living costs?

(I need to confirm they are NOT paying, as you generally cannot get Child Benefit if the council is financially supporting the child)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Since the council is not paying for Alex, you meet all the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**For your son Alex**, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

## Why You're Eligible:

Alex qualifies under the **20-week extension period** because he:
- Is 17 years old (within the 16-19 age range)
- Left school approximately 2 months ago (well within the 20-week window)
- Has registered with the local careers service (a qualifying service)
- Is not working 24+ hours per week
- Does not receive benefits in his own right
- Is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship

And you meet the claimant requirements:
- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- Alex lives with you (demonstrating responsibility)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- The local council is not paying towards his care

## Important Reminder:
The 20-week extension will end either:
- **20 weeks after Alex left school**, OR
- **When Alex turns 18** (whichever comes first)

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments:** Made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about claiming or the extension period?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit under the 20-week extension rule. He is 17 years old (aged 16-19), recently left school approximately 2 months ago (within the 20-week window), and has registered with the local careers service which qualifies as an approved service. He is not working (less than 24 hours per week), does not receive benefits in his own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), Alex lives with the claimant demonstrating responsibility, no one else is claiming for Alex, he is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex under the 20-week extension period for young people who have left education and registered with a careers service. All other eligibility requirements are satisfied.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit under the 20-week extension rule. He is 17 years old (aged 16-19), recently left school approximately 2 months ago (within the 20-week window), and has registered with the local careers service which qualifies as an approved service. He is not working (less than 24 hours per week), does not receive benefits in his own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), Alex lives with the claimant demonstrating responsibility, no one else is claiming for Alex, he is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex under the 20-week extension period for young people who have left education and registered with a careers service. All other eligibility requirements are satisfied.
